title: 09_sequential_awaits_loop.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Fetch the Dashboard in One Round-Trip

The dashboard loads a user's followed projects by first fetching the list of project IDs, then fetching details for each project individually. On a user following 8 projects the page takes over 2 seconds to render — far above the 500ms target. The network waterfall shows 8 sequential requests: each one waits for the previous to finish before starting.

The project IDs are independent — there is no reason to wait for project #1 before fetching project #2. All 8 requests could be in flight simultaneously, and the total time would be the duration of the slowest single request, not the sum of all of them.

Replace the sequential loop with a strategy that fires all requests concurrently and waits for all of them to settle before rendering. Handle the case where one individual fetch fails without breaking the entire dashboard.
traceback:
PERF  dashboard.test.jsx > loads all 8 projects within 500ms

  TimeoutError: Dashboard took 2240ms to render all projects.

  Network waterfall:
    GET /api/users/u1/project-ids   →  50ms
    GET /api/projects/p1            → 280ms  (waited for ids)
    GET /api/projects/p2            → 310ms  (waited for p1)
    GET /api/projects/p3            → 290ms  (waited for p2)
    ...8 sequential requests total → 2240ms

  Each project request waited for the previous one to finish.
correctOutput:
PERF  dashboard.test.jsx > loads all 8 projects within 500ms

  Dashboard rendered in 342ms.

  Network waterfall:
    GET /api/users/u1/project-ids  →  50ms
    GET /api/projects/p1..p8       → 290ms  (all 8 in parallel)
  Total: 342ms

options.label: Map the IDs to fetch Promises and resolve them concurrently with Promise.allSettled
options.resultLog:
PERF  dashboard.test.jsx > loads all 8 projects within 500ms

  Dashboard rendered in 342ms.

  Network waterfall:
    GET /api/users/u1/project-ids  →  50ms
    GET /api/projects/p1..p8       → 290ms  (all 8 in parallel)
  Total: 342ms
options.rationale: `ids.map(id => fetch(...))` creates all 8 Promises simultaneously before any of them has resolved — they are all in flight at once. `Promise.allSettled` waits for every Promise to settle (either fulfill or reject) and returns an array of result objects with a `status` field. Filtering to `"fulfilled"` entries renders whatever loaded successfully; a single failed project fetch does not blank the entire dashboard. The concurrency win is real: 8 independent 300ms requests become ~300ms total instead of ~2400ms.

options.label: Use Promise.all instead of a loop — reject the entire load if any project fetch fails
options.resultLog:
PERF  dashboard.test.jsx > loads all 8 projects within 500ms
  Dashboard rendered in 338ms. ✓

  FAIL  dashboard.test.jsx > shows available projects when one fetch fails
  The load() function threw — setProjects never called — dashboard blank.
  Promise.all rejects immediately when any single fetch rejects.
options.rationale: `Promise.all` achieves the same concurrency win — all 8 requests in parallel — and the performance test passes. But `Promise.all` rejects as soon as *any* Promise in the array rejects ("fail-fast" behavior), propagating the error to the `load()` caller. In the partial-failure test, a single unavailable project causes the whole dashboard to go blank rather than showing the 7 that loaded fine. For a dashboard that should show whatever is available, `Promise.allSettled` is the right choice: it waits for all Promises regardless of individual failures and gives you per-item results.

options.label: Batch the requests 3 at a time using a loop over chunks of the IDs array
options.resultLog:
PERF  dashboard.test.jsx > loads all 8 projects within 500ms

  Dashboard rendered in 910ms — 3 batches of sequential parallel chunks.

  Network waterfall:
    Batch 1 (p1,p2,p3):  300ms in parallel
    Batch 2 (p4,p5,p6):  310ms in parallel  (waited for batch 1)
    Batch 3 (p7,p8):     295ms in parallel  (waited for batch 2)
  Total: 910ms — still exceeds 500ms target.
options.rationale: Chunked batching is the right pattern when you need to rate-limit requests to a server with strict concurrency limits or when firing all N requests simultaneously would overwhelm a resource. Neither condition applies here — the API handles concurrent requests fine, and the IDs are few. Batching 8 independent requests into 3 serial groups of 3 turns a single ~300ms parallel round-trip into three ~300ms serial round-trips, totaling ~900ms. Always start with full concurrency; apply batching only when there is a concrete rate-limit or resource constraint to respect.

hints: An `await` inside a `for...of` loop pauses the loop until each Promise resolves before moving to the next iteration. That turns independent requests into sequential ones. What built-in function takes an *array of Promises* and waits for all of them to settle concurrently?

Lines 9–12: each `await fetch(...)` inside the loop waits for the previous fetch before starting the next. Replace the loop with `ids.map(id => fetch(...))` to launch all Promises at once, then use `Promise.allSettled(promises)` to wait for all of them simultaneously.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`await` inside a `for...of` loop is sequential by construction: the loop body pauses at `await fetch(...)` until that Promise resolves, then moves to the next iteration. For 8 requests each taking ~280ms, that is 8 × 280ms ≈ 2240ms. The requests have no dependency on each other — project #2's fetch does not need project #1's result — so this sequencing is pure waste.

### Why the fix is correct
`ids.map(id => fetch(...).then(r => r.json()))` evaluates the map synchronously, launching all 8 fetch calls before any of them resolves. The result is an array of 8 in-flight Promises. `Promise.allSettled(promises)` then waits for every Promise in the array to settle — fulfill or reject — and returns an array of `{ status, value }` / `{ status, reason }` result objects. Total time: the duration of the slowest individual request (~300ms) plus the time to fetch the ID list (~50ms), for ~350ms total.

`allSettled` is preferred over `all` for partial-failure resilience: if project #7 is temporarily unavailable, the other 7 still load and the dashboard renders them. `Promise.all` would reject the entire load on the first failure.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and the right choice of `allSettled` over `all`. The performance test passing is the obvious bar, but the partial-failure test is what separates the production-quality answer: a dashboard that goes blank when one of eight endpoints has a transient error is worse than one that shows seven items and silently omits the eighth. The batching distractor is good at demonstrating when *not* to batch — recognizing that chunking 8 independent requests into 3 serial groups is an optimization for a constraint that doesn't exist here shows you reason about tradeoffs rather than reaching for a pattern because you've seen it. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Concurrency in JavaScript: await in Loops vs. Promise.all

JavaScript is single-threaded but its async model allows many I/O operations to be in flight simultaneously. Understanding when `await` serializes work and when to use Promise combinators to parallelize it is one of the highest-leverage performance skills in async JavaScript.

### Why await in a loop is sequential
When the JavaScript engine encounters `await expression`, it suspends the current async function and schedules the rest to resume after the awaited Promise settles. In a `for...of` loop, this means:
1. Start iteration 1 — await the fetch — **pause**
2. Resume when fetch 1 resolves — push result — start iteration 2 — await — **pause**
3. ...

Each iteration waits for the previous one before starting. Total time: sum of all request durations. For independent requests, this is maximally inefficient.

### Promise.all — concurrent, fail-fast
`Promise.all(promises)` takes an iterable of Promises, starts them all immediately (they were already started by `map` — `Promise.all` just tracks them), and resolves with an array of values in the same order as the input when *all* have fulfilled. If *any* rejects, `Promise.all` rejects immediately with that reason, leaving the other Promises to continue running but discarding their results.

```js
const results = await Promise.all(ids.map(id => fetchProject(id)));
// total time ≈ max(individual times), not sum
```

Use `Promise.all` when: (a) all results are required and (b) a single failure should fail the whole operation.

### Promise.allSettled — concurrent, partial results
`Promise.allSettled(promises)` waits for every Promise to settle regardless of individual failures, then resolves with an array of result descriptors:
```js
[
  { status: 'fulfilled', value: projectData },
  { status: 'rejected', reason: Error('404') },
  ...
]
```

Filter to `'fulfilled'` entries and map their `.value` to get all successfully-loaded results. Use `allSettled` when some failures are acceptable and you want to show whatever succeeded — dashboards, lists, galleries.

### Controlled concurrency: batching
Sometimes you *do* want to limit concurrency — when the server enforces rate limits, when you are hitting a third-party API with a per-second cap, or when firing 1000 requests simultaneously would OOM the server. The chunked-loop pattern:
```js
const BATCH = 5;
for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i += BATCH) {
  const chunk = items.slice(i, i + BATCH);
  await Promise.all(chunk.map(processItem));
}
```
This fires at most `BATCH` requests in parallel, waits for each batch to complete, then starts the next. Apply this only when there is a real constraint to respect — don't reach for it by default.

### Promise.race and Promise.any
- `Promise.race` — resolves/rejects with the first settled Promise. Useful for timeouts.
- `Promise.any` — resolves with the first fulfilled Promise, rejects only if all reject. Useful for redundant sources.

### Common variations of this bug
- `async` functions called inside `Array.forEach` — `forEach` ignores returned Promises; use `map` then `Promise.all`.
- `for...of` with `await` where the loop result does not depend on the previous iteration — always a candidate for `Promise.all`.
- Chained `.then()` calls that each return a new fetch — these also serialize; restructure with `Promise.all`.
- `await` inside a `reduce` accumulator — produces a chain of sequential operations; restructure as `map` + `Promise.all` then a final `reduce`.
